coredata.dc:title: A carbon-based fluorescent probe (N-CDs) encapsulated in a zeolite matrix (NaFZ) for ultrasensitive detection of Hg (II) in fish
coredata.dc:creator: Othman, Hazha Omar
Hassan, Rebwar
Faizullah, Azad
Ghadiri, M.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel strategy was addressed to fabricate new sensing probe (N-CDs@NaFZ) from nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) confined in Al-free ferrisilicates zeolite (NaFZ) by hydrothermal/solvothermal method. The probe was systematically characterized by HR-TEM, FTIR, energy dispersive X-ray (EDX), powder X-ray diffraction, and UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectrophotometers. Characterization of the designed nanocomposite template N-CDs@NaFZ by fluorescence spectrum demonstrates a variety of important conducts as stability improvements, reasonable dispersibility in water, highly emission intensity enhancement at 435 nm when excited at 340 nm, excitation independent fluorescence behaviors, great quantum yield percentage of 91.2%, and narrow size distribution 12 nm, as a nano-space confinement effect of zeolite effectively increase the rigidity of N-CDs. Based on the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the designed approach exhibits an excellent selectivity and good sensitive response to the presence of Hg(II) ions under ambient temperature, with a wide linear range of 0.1–1500 nM and lower detection limits of 5.5 pM. Influences of variables pH and incubation time were optimized. The N-CDs@NaFZ sensor was effectively applied for the detection of Hg(II) ions in the farmed and wild rainbow trout fishes, and the results are in reasonable agreement when compared with that obtained by the cold vapor atomic absorption method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hydrothermal
Zeolite
Hg(II) ions detection
Fluorescence quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Hg(II) and calibration curve

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Chemistry - College of Science - Salahaddin University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author would like to thanks all stuff members of Department of Chemistry - College of Science - Salahaddin University -Erbil for their helping during research interval, which in so many ways were involved in the present work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of N-CDs@NaFZ nanocomposite: HR-TEM image (a), scale distribution histogram (inset), SEM image (b), and EDX analysis (c).
Comparison analysis between NaFZ zeolite, N-CDs@NaFZ nanocomposite, and N-CDs carbon dots by XRD pattern (a) and FTIR (b).

a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, N-CDs@NaFZ, and NaFZ (the inset graph is N-CDs@NaFZ in the absence and presence of 100 nm Hg(II); b) Fluorescence spectrum of nanocomposite (N-CDs@NaFZ) excited from 300 to 400 nm, with 20 nm wavelength intervals; c) Excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs and N-CDs@NaFZ; d) Fluorescence spectra displayed quenching effects of 1000 nM Hg(II) ions.
Sensor signal optimization using experimental variables: a) pH effect and b) incubation time effect.

(a) Fluorescence responses of N-CDs@NaFZ in the presence of different concentrations of Hg(II) ions and (b) The linear relation curve between Fo/F and the concentration of Hg(II) ions (Fo and F represent fluorescence intensities at 435 nm in the presence and absence of Hg(II) ions, the inset graph depicts the concentration of Hg (II) ions in the range (0.1–100 nM).
Effect of various metal ions (1000 nM) on the fluorescence intensity of newly N-CDs@NaFZ probe.
Comparison of the analysis quality among several previously published studies using fluorescence sensors and the present sensor to detect Hg(II) ions.
Results for the determination of Hg(II) ions in various Rainbow trout fish samples obtained (wild and farmed).

row.entry: Detectable amount of Hg(II) (μg/g) a ± SD

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a)
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The results are the mean of analysis of 5 fish with five replicates (n = 5) for each type of sample (wild and farmed).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b)
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Cold vapor Atomic absorption spectroscopy method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d)
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Tabulate Students t-test and variances F test at 95% confidence limit were 2.228 and 2.98, respectively.

head.ce:title: A carbon-based fluorescent probe (N-CDs) encapsulated in a zeolite matrix (NaFZ) for ultrasensitive detection of Hg (II) in fish

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, College of Science, Salahaddin University-Erbil, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, College of Science, Salahaddin University-Erbil, Iraq

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Inorganic Chemistry Department, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, C.P. 51664, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Inorganic Chemistry Department, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, C.P. 51664, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel strategy was addressed to fabricate new sensing probe (N-CDs@NaFZ) from nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) confined in Al-free ferrisilicates zeolite (NaFZ) by hydrothermal/solvothermal method. The probe was systematically characterized by HR-TEM, FTIR, energy dispersive X-ray (EDX), powder X-ray diffraction, and UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectrophotometers. Characterization of the designed nanocomposite template N-CDs@NaFZ by fluorescence spectrum demonstrates a variety of important conducts as stability improvements, reasonable dispersibility in water, highly emission intensity enhancement at 435 nm when excited at 340 nm, excitation independent fluorescence behaviors, great quantum yield percentage of 91.2%, and narrow size distribution 12 nm, as a nano-space confinement effect of zeolite effectively increase the rigidity of N-CDs. Based on the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the designed approach exhibits an excellent selectivity and good sensitive response to the presence of Hg(II) ions under ambient temperature, with a wide linear range of 0.1–1500 nM and lower detection limits of 5.5 pM. Influences of variables pH and incubation time were optimized. The N-CDs@NaFZ sensor was effectively applied for the detection of Hg(II) ions in the farmed and wild rainbow trout fishes, and the results are in reasonable agreement when compared with that obtained by the cold vapor atomic absorption method.

ce:section.ce:para: The critical global issue nowadays is environmental pollution due to heavy metal ions and bacteria [1–4]. Mercury ions as a heavy metal are toxic environmental pollutants resulting from natural and anthropogenic impact. Additionally, it's widely widespread in water and the lithosphere. Hygienically, mercury has a serious and dangerous effect on human health unless in very low concentrations. The highest acceptable level of mercury ions in drinking water and edible fish are 2.0 ppb and 0.55 ppm respectively, according to the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [5–8]. The human health risks of mercury as a result of exposures and/or consumption of contaminated drinking water, foods [9,10], practice use in labs, dental amalgams [11–13] and vaccine [14,15] become serious and influential, especially when the aquatic ecosystem microorganisms convert mercury ions into methylmercury that can be bioaccumulate in human body by food chain [16–18]. This element is readily able to cause several diseases such as kidney failure, parental brain damage, and serious damage to the central nervous system and immune system disfunction [19]. In light of this, the literature review reveals that many analytical techniques have been developed for the determination of this element with different methodology, including electrochemical method [20], atomic absorption and emission spectroscopy [21], colorimetric [22], surface enhanced Raman scattering and inductive coupled plasma mass spectrometry [23–25]. Many of these techniques suffer from some limitations associated with complicated sample preparation protocols, sophisticated instruments, time-consuming and professional training for operation. Among these methods, the fluorometric method of detection has several advantages involving selectivity, high sensitivity, simple operation and application, wide range and fast response [26]. This makes development of a new fluorescence probe for Hg(II) sensing, specialist and applicable to the environmental and biological milieus as a necessary technological support for Hg(II) ions contamination [27].

Currently, nanocrystals of carbon materials that are called carbon dots (CDs), also called fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs), carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) or carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are more popular research topics and have received extensive attraction. Nanocarbon materials are composed of carbogenic cores with functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine … etc.) and dimensions of less than 10 nm. It has many characteristic properties including different size reliant optical properties like fluorescence and electrochemical fields, aqueous solubility, biocompatibility, great elasticity in modification, and high resistance against photobleaching chemical inertness. They have found numerous application fields like bioimaging, catalysis, drug delivery, chemical sensing. As sensing sources, several groups of chemicals have been successfully analyzed using CDs-based sensors including bacteria, nucleic acid and protein, small organic molecules, pesticides, explosives and metal ions [28].

All these features and characteristics of these CDs encouraged researchers to work hard to develop everything related to them. The current orientation of the researchers is to fix these CDs particles in different host matrices with a stable structure and composition such as polymers, zeolites and other matrices that have a solid structure and can control the size of the CDs during the synthesis process, as they containing holes nanoscale sizes. As a result, a new type of CDs confined into matrices materials has evolved. Thus, the resulting nanocomposite acquires distinct properties, including phosphorescence, afterglow, increased fluorescence intensity, and high stability [29–31].

Zeolite, related to tectosilicate family minerals and crystalline nanoporous compounds, has a number of interesting properties in shape, topology, pore size, framework composition, and stability toward high pressure and temperature. Therefore, zeolite in the preparation of nano-carbon materials during different synthesis conditions and procedures can serve as hard templates and an ideal candidate incorporates functional guests [32]. The CD@zeolite composites have recently shown tunable fluorescence properties and elevated quantum yields. The CDs ambient by zeolite exhibit an efficient emission and lifetime. This is attributed to the effective stabilization of the triplet excited state (T) and suppresses the radiationless process by intersystem crossing of the CDs. Additionally, hindered oxygen quenching and hydroxyl groups in the framework of zeolites could form hydrogen bonds with the CO and CN functional groups of CDs [31,33].

In the present work, we developed a new synthesis strategy for the preparation of N-CDs (using citric acid and urea as a precursor) confined in ferrisilicate zeolites matrices with ZSM-5 structure by hydrothermal method. The synthesized N-CDs@NaFZ nanocomposite is characterized and exhibits unprecedented fluorescence emission and high quantum yield. It has been observed that the light emitted by N-CDs@NaFZ is effectively suppressed in the presence of Hg(II) ions, with which determination of Hg(II) ions in fish real samples can be accomplished.

All chemicals used were of analytical grade and were used as received without any further purification. Citric acid and urea were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Silicic acid, iron (III) nitrate, sodium carbonate, potassium carbonate, sulfuric acid and tetrapropylammonium bromide (TPABr) were purchased from Merck Co. The others chemicals like NaCl, KCl, AgNO3, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, PbCl2, CuSO4·5H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, MnCl2·H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O and AlCl3·6H2O are obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Acetate buffer solution (pH 7.0) was prepared from sodium acetate (0.01 M) and concentrated acetic acid. Mercury stock solution (10.0 mM) was prepared from Hg(NO3)2 salt (Merck). While lower concentrations were prepared by serial dilution of the stock solution with the acetate buffer of pH 7.0.

The UV–vis absorbance spectra were recorded using a Lambda 25 absorption spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, US). Fluorescence measurements were carried out by using a PerkinElmer LS-45 fluorescence spectrometer (Buckinghamshire, UK). HRTEM images were taken using a transmission electron microscope (PHILIPS, CM120, Netherland). SEM, EDS image was obtained by TESCAN MIRA III (Czech). FTIR spectra were recorded by the Perkin –Elmer spectrometer at room temperature. The XRD pattern was done with the use of a Philips instrument (PW, 1730).

The NaFZ zeolite sample was prepared, as described in our previous paper [34], in a non-stirred autoclave by hydrothermal procedure from silicic acid, iron (III) nitrate, sodium carbonate, sulfuric acid, and tetrapropylammonium bromide (TPABr) as template. The molar ratios between the components was Si/Fe = 15, TPABr/Si = 0.17, H2O/Si = 35 and Na/Si = 2. For Si/Fe (input) = 15, the corresponding Si/Fe molar ratio was 17.04. Firstly, the solution of silicic acid and sodium carbonate in distilled water was added slowly to the solution of iron (III) nitrate and after adding TPABr template crystallization was done in an autoclave at 550 °C for 5 h.

The selection of the synthesis method was based on facilitating seamless access of N-CDs into the zeolite matrix that provides maximum loading and high product yield. In this procedure, 0.3 g of the preprepared zeolite with 1.0 g of citric acid and 0.5 g of urea is ground for 10 min, and then dissolved in 15 mL deionized water. The mixture solution is stirred for 24 h at room temperature. The blended solution was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 2 days in an oven. Then it left to cool down to room temperature slowly. The synthesized N-CDs@NaFZ is sonicated for 30 min and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for further 10 min, then washed by deionized water several times. For more purification, the resultant product dialyzes for 24 h using a dialysis membrane (Molecular-weight cutoff = 500). The product is left to dry overnight at room temperature to obtain 0.421 g. The as-synthesized nanocomposite's quantum yield was calculated and found to be 91.2% (detail can be found in supplementary information).
For the intent of comparison, the N-CDs were prepared in accordance with previously published report, with minor modification [35].

The Hg(II) ion detection was performed at room temperature in acetate buffer pH = 7 for evaluation the sensitivity of method towards Hg(II) ion. Typically, to prepare a 50.0 μg/mL stock solution of purified N-CDs@NaFZ nanocomposite as stock solution, 0.1 g of the nanocomposite diluted by deionized water to 2 L volumetric flask and stored at 4 °C for subsequent work. A 4.0 μL of prepared nanocomposite solution was mixed with different volume of Hg(II) standard solution in 4.0 mL flask. The resulting mixture was then diluted to the mark with deionized water and mixed rigorously. The fluorescence spectra were recorded after 5 min of mixing at 340 nm excitation and 435 nm emission wavelengths with 5 nm slit width.

This study was conducted in Iran, where the consumption of this type of fish (rainbow trout) has an ancient history, and in addition to the fishing operations, there are farms where this type of fish is raised [36] On this basis, this type of fish was selected in this work. Based on a previously published paper, two rainbow trout fish sources, wild (naturally fed) and farmd (fed with commercial pellet), in two separate locations for each type were chosen for the analysis of Hg(II) ions in this study [37].
After beheading and evisceration, the fish samples from each of the trout farms or fishing sites were packed in polyethylene pouches and stored at −20 °C prior to analysis.

Frozen rainbow trout samples were left for 1 h to thaw at room temperature before the digestion process begins. Fish muscles were removed from the spinal cord and sliced, then cut into very small pieces using a clean stainless-steel knife on a clean polyethylene surface. The excess of water was excluded with filter paper. Approximately 40.0 g of fish fillet is homogenized in a mortar and fish paste was obtained, from which the Hg(II) ions were extracted after digestion process.

Digestion procedure of fish sample was performed as described by Louie [38] with some modification. In detail, 2.0 g of fish sample was weight in a 250 mL Erlenmeyer flask. Then, 10 mL of acid mixture (5 mL nitric acid 100%, 3 mL sulfuric acid 98% and 2 mL HCl 37%) was added to the flask. The flask covered by watch glass and mixed for 20 min. Then the flask placed on a water bath at 100 °C and sonicated for 1 h then left to cool down to room temperature. The remaining solution of prepared sample (4.0 mL) diluted to 50 mL in volumetric flask by deionized water. Mercury present in the fish sample, as methyl mercury, during digestion procedure applied for fish sample it will be totally converted Hg(II) due to the strong oxidant action [39].

There are three points to consider when developing a CDs@Zeolite nanocomposite synthesis strategy; firstly, existence of heteroatoms (N, C, O and P) in CDs raw materials [40]. Secondly, the zeolite should provide strong hydrogen bonding or strong covalent bond to tightly confine CDs. Thirdly, synthetic conditions employed to favor the maximum embedding of CDs in to zeolite matrix. A new N-CD@ziolite nanocomposite was synthesized based on the previous information, and characterization studies were conducted to verify its new composition.

The SEM analysis was used to investigate the morphology and crystalline size of zeolite (NaFZ) before confining with carbon dotes. The analysis image in Fig. S1a demonstrates that the NaFZ particles were in regular surface and composed of small nano size crystals. The TEM image in Fig. S1b shows the characterized nanoparticles has nearly spherical and uniform particle morphology with 5–15 nm size distribution range of particles [34]. Additionally, the image explains that the zeolite particles are homogenously well dispersed. Likewise, Fig. S1c is HRTEM image for N-CDs alone which show that it was spherical in shape (4 nm diameters) and good dispersibility in aqueous medium [35].

The morphologies and particle size for as-synthesized N-CDs@NaFZ nanocomposite was performed by different HRTEM images and the size distribution was calculated by using image J software. The images in Fig. 1 a and Fig. S2 predicate that the nanocomposite has a spherical structure, indicating that the shape of NaFZ remains the same even after the N-CDs are implanted into their pores. It reveals that instead of adsorbing on the crystalline surface, N-CDs were found inside the crystalline structure of NaFZ particles. The particle size distribution histogram of N-CDs@NaFZ demonstrates maximum particle size was 12 nm. The SEM image in Fig. 1b support this fabrication procedure and well confining N-CDs in NaFZ zeolite nano particles. Elemental analysis was conducted by Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) for further characterization to predicate the nanocomposite composition, as shown in Fig. 1c, the elemental analysis and mapping inset of N-CDs@NaFZ. The weight percentage of C, N, O, Na, Si and Fe was found to be 39.21, 24.22, 35.04, 1.08, 0.14 and 0.32 respectively which confirm that N-CDs@NaFZ nanocomposite is good synthesized with presence of N-CDs in to the pore of the matrix.

The XRD pattern in Fig. 2 a, for NaFZ zeolite shows good agreement with XRD patterns of ZSM-5 in literature, as the characterized peaks at 2Ѳ = 7.92°, 8.84°, 23.13°, 23.8° representing (011), (020), (051) and (033) planes of crystal structure respectively [41]. In the pattern of as-prepared N-CDs@NaFZ in Fig. 2a it's clear that the addition N-CDs to the NaFZ zeolite has no effect on the main crystalline structure as those zeolite peaks are remain viewable in XRD patterns. The small broadness that appear at 2Ѳ = 20ᶱ represent the N-CDs particles embedded in NaFZ matrix. In the same vein, the broad diffraction peak centered at 2Ѳ = 20ᶱ, related to a turbostratic of carbon phase. The obtained result was in good agreement with that of Othman et al. as well as Lu and his group, who reported in a similar manner. The lack of a sharp peak in the XRD pattern of N-CDs affirms the amorphous nature of the nanoparticles [42].

Additional characterization of N-CDs, N-CDs@NaFZ and NaFZ were accomplished through FTIR spectroscopy and the obtained spectra are included in Fig. 2b. The FTIR spectrum of NaFZ reveals some distinct bands that are related to the NaFZ sample. The 650 cm−1 band was associated with the iron-containing sample and more specifically, the Si–O–Fe bond group in aluminum-free NaFZ zeolite. At 547 cm−1, the second characteristic band for the pentasil structure's five membered ring (double ring vibration) was clearly observed, as was the bending vibration of the T–O–T (T = Fe or Si) band at 450 cm−1. External linkages between TO4 tetrahedral could be responsible for the T–O bond's asymmetric stretching vibration at 1224 cm−1 [34]. While FTIR spectrum of the N-CDs, there appears a number of characteristic bands related to the surface functional groups. Among them, bands at 3430 cm−1 for O–H stretching vibration, 1648 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration peak of N–H, band at 2900 cm−1 for C–H vibration and CO bond at 1750 cm−1. Another band at 1510 cm−1 for N–H and 1390 cm−1 is for C–N bonds [43]. Because of the precursors used in N-CD synthesis, these findings confirm that CDs have both carboxylic and amine groups on their surfaces. In the FTIR spectrum of as-synthesized N-CDs@NaFZ nanocomposite, all of the characteristic bands of both N-CDs and NaFZ can be seen, confirming the formation of embedding process Fig. 2b.

In Fig. 3 a, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of N-CDs reveal two maximum absorption peaks at 236 nm and 340 nm, which correspond to the π-π* transition of CC bond and the n-π* transition of CO bond. Same absorption peaks can be observed in N-CDs@NaFZ nanocomposite. The effect of Hg (II) absence and presence is depicted in the inset graph in Fig. 3a. The inclusion of Hg(II) causes a decrease in light absorption at 340 nm and the appearance of a new shoulder in the UV region of 272. The absorption and emission spectra of the fabricated N-CDs@NaFZ probe are shown in Fig. S3. The size distribution can explain whether a large absorption band rather than an apparent absorption peak is observed [44]. These indicate that the nanocomposite has been successfully formed, as no absorption signal was observed for NaFZ. Excitation independent emission wavelength for N-CDs@NaFZ nanocomposite was recognized in the fluorescence study, as shown in Fig. 3b, when different emission spectra in the range 300–400 nm were recorded for N-CDs@NaFZ with excitation wavelength intervals of 20 nm [45]. As such, with a 340 nm excitation wavelength, the optimal fluorescence intensity was observed at wavelength 435 nm. In Fig. 3c, the excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs and N-CDs@NaFZ are manifest, and it is apparent that the carbon dot in both spectra has the same excitation (340 nm) and emission (435 nm) wavelengths, with varying degrees of intensity. Additionally, when contrasted to the light emissions obtained from both N-CDs and N-CDs @NaFZ, zeolite (NaFZ) had no emissions.

To demonstrate the sensitivity and sensing ability of the prepared N-CDs@NaFZ toward Hg(II) ions, two solutions of the same concentration of N-CDs@NaFZ and N-CDs were prepared, and the fluorescence intensity were measured before and after addition of 1000 nM Hg(II) ions. The differences in fluorescence intensity ΔF (Fo-F) of N-CDs@NaFZ in the presence (F) and absence (Fo) of Hg(II) ions are higher than that of N-CDs, according to the comparison results Fig. 3d. This demonstrates that the nanocomposite (N-CDs@NaFZ) has a high sensitivity and good capability as a sensing probe.

The impact of sensor conditions as pH and time of incubation on the quenching reaction between N-CDs@NaFZ and Hg(II) ions, as well as the fluorescence intensity, were investigated. In the presence and absence of 100 nM Hg ions, the fluorescence response and quenching degree was studied at different pH [4,6–8,10]. Generally, different pH values have a distinct impact on fluorescence intensity due to their effect on the luminescence source (N-CDs@NaFZ) and the analyte sample (Hg(II)), resulting in lower quenching efficiency (Fo/F). Fig. 4 a shows that the amount of quenching intensity is low at low pH values, increases to its highest value when pH = 7, and then decreases with increasing pH. Functional groups such as amine, hydroxyl, and carboxylic acid coat the surface of the N-CDs@NaFZ; a change in pH can change the charge of these groups, impairing their interaction with Hg(II) ions. In another way, at higher pH, hydroxide ions can form coordination complexes with Hg(II) ions, causing the quenching ability to change [27,46–50]. Maximum quenching efficiency (Fo/F) obtained at pH = 7 thus it selected for subsequent works. The incubation time, or the time required for complete reaction between N-CDs@NaFZ nanocomposite and Hg(II) ions by monitoring the fluorescence efficiency, was also evaluated at different time intervals (1–120 min). As shown in Fig. 4b, the quenching reaction of the N-CDs@NaFZ by 100 nM Hg(II) began after 1 min, reached its maximum after 5 min, and then remained relatively stable for another 120 min. We conclude that the quenching reaction was relatively fast, taking only 5 min to complete and chosen as best time for incubation time.

Fluorescence probes can form intermolecular charge-transfer states and the mechanism of photoinduced electron transfer, when an excited electron is transferred from donor N-CDS@NaFZ to acceptor Hg(II) ions in an excited state electron transfer operation. This process has been used as a basis to develop chemosensor for these metal ions. Quenching was caused by the interaction of Hg(II) ions with active functional groups on the surface of N-CDs such as –OH and –COOH. As a result of these interactions, an electron in the excited state was transferred to the Hg(II) ions' empty d orbitals, forming coordination bonds and complexes [51–55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hg(II) and calibration curve
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the sensitivity and applicability of the sensing probe of as-synthesized N-CDs@NaFZ toward Hg(II) ions, the fluorescence spectra of the nanocomposite material in the presence of different concentrations of mercury ions were reported in Fig. 5 a. It was obvious that as the concentration of Hg(II) ions was increased, the fluorescence intensity at 435 nm was progressively reduced because of that Stern-Volmer equation is applied [56].

As can be shown from Fig. 5b, a good linear correlation was obtained by plotting the quenching efficiency (Fo/F) against different concentrations of Hg(II) ions in the range 0.1–1500 nM. The obtained linear regression equation is Fo/F = 0.0084C + 1.109 (where Fo and F represent, respectively, the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs@NaFZ in the absence and presence of Hg(II) ions and C is the concentration of Hg(II) ions), with corresponding regression coefficient R2 equal to 0.9971. According to the quenching efficiency curve slope (Ks) and standard deviation (SDb) of blank solution (n = 5), the detection limit (LOD = 3SDb/Ks) was calculated to be 5.5 pM when the signal-to-noise ratio is equal to 3.

The competence of the chemosensor was statistically evaluated by assessing precision and accuracy of the method. The precision was determined by five repetitive analyses (n = 5) of different Hg(II) standard solutions under the aforementioned optimum conditions. While the accuracy of the method was determined by measuring the relative error (%E) and the results were tabulated in Table S1. The satisfactory results obtained indicate that the established method has good precision and accuracy.

In terms of linear range and detection limit, this sensing approach outperforms other observed fluorescence methods on the subject of sensitivity, lower detection limit, and wide linear range for Hg(II) determination Table 1 . Furthermore, this method is facile, environmentally friendly, no dangerous and harmful organic substances used during synthesis process, fast in response and stable.
Detection system Results Detection limit (pM) Response range (nM) Type of sample Ref.
Fluorescence Quenching (FRET) 250 1–10000 Fish [5]
Fluorescence FRET 15,000 20–800 Serum [57]
Fluorescence FRET 490 1–10 Tap water [58]
Fluorescence Quenching 10,000 15–250 – [59]
Fluorescence Quenching 2910 0–50 Tap and mineral water [27]
Fluorescence Quenching 3000 10–50000 water [60]
Fluorescence Quenching 500 0.5–10 Lake water [61]
Fluorescence Quenching 5.5 0.1–1500
This work

Selectivity is one of the most important criteria for any method effectiveness, and it is just as important as sensitivity. In light of this, the effect of a number of metal ions (Na+, K+, Ag+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Al3+ and Hg(II)) on the intensity of fluorescence was encountered by measuring the extent of its ability to extinguish emitted fluorescence light. This study was conducted in two steps: Firstly, we studied the effect of each related metal (100 nM) separately on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@NaFZ. With the exception of Hg(II), the effect of other ions can be neglected, concluding that N-CDs@NaFZ can be employed to detect Hg(II) selectively Fig. 6 . Secondly, to investigate the impact of these ions on the ability of Hg(II) to quench the fluorescence light of the N-CDs@NaFZ, 100 nM of each ion were applied to a mixture of Hg(II) N-CDs@NaFZ under optimum experimental conditions. We find that these ions have almost no distinct effect on the quenching susceptibility of Hg(II), as shown in Fig. S4a. The combined effects of these ions on the strength of fluorescence, the quenching ability of Hg(II) and cross reactivity were assessed further. A mixture of ions with a concentration of 100 nM per ion was applied to this fluorescent approach and displayed in Fig. S4b-c. The results of this study show that these ions have no major impact on the sensing process, allowing it to be labeled as a selective method for Hg(II) ion determination. This high selectivity of the sensor probe for mercury ions can be explained from several views. Including the high ability of mercury ions compared to other ions to withdraw electrons and form complexes and coordination bonds with functional groups that contain oxygen on the carbon dots surface with higher stability constants [60,61]. Moreover, non-radiative recombination of exactions is induced by an effective electron transfer mechanism from the donor (N-CDs) to the acceptor (Hg(II)), resulting in a distinct Fluorescence quenching effect [27].

Fish samples were used to test the efficacy of the fluorescence probe (N-CDs@NaFZ) for detecting Hg(II) in real samples. Where under optimum experimental conditions, the probe was applied successfully for quantitative determination of Hg(II) in different fish samples and the results were tabulated in Table 2 . The results obtained by this method are compared with that using cold vapor atomic absorption spectroscopy (CVAAS) and no significance differences were observed. Additionally, to predict the effect of the sample matrix, A pre-analyzed fish sample was spiked with different concentrations of Hg(II) ions, and the results were summarized in Table S2. Indicating that The new methods have great potential for their relatively high sensitivity and selectivity in determination of Hg(II) in real samples.

In a nutshell, the new approach has been improved as a consequence of two variables discussed in the method. A sensitive and selective sensing probe has been innovated, which includes nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) encapsulating by ferrisilicate zeolite (NaFZ). A simple hydrothermal approach has been used during fabrication procedure. The probes show great fluorescence intensity at 435 nm by irradiation with UV light. By quenching fluorescence light, the N-CDS@NaFZ showed a strong response to the presence of Hg(II) ions. The newly developed sensor has a wide linear range and a low detection limit, making it a possible tool for estimation Hg(II) ions. In another light, depending on the essential requirements of high-quality, balanced seafood items is an important part of balancing the human diet. As one of the heavy metals that causes significant health problems, this improvement method provides details on Hg(II) metal accumulation in farmed and wild rainbow trout.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author would like to thanks all stuff members of Department of Chemistry - College of Science - Salahaddin University -Erbil for their helping during research interval, which in so many ways were involved in the present work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2021.122646.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Critical soil concentrations of cadmium, lead, and mercury in view of health effects on humans and animals
